coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanocomposite for photo-degradation of Rhodamine B and cefradine
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiao
Shu, Juan
Anqi, Zhang
Juyuan, Heng
Yan, Zhengyu
Chen, Jianqiu
coredata.dc:description: Pure TiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-doped TiO2 nanocomposite (CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite) were prepared by a sol-gel approach for photocatalytic removal of Rhodamine B and cefradine. Analyses by Transmission electronmicroscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS), UV–visible spectroscopy and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) confirmed the successful formation of CQDs/TiO2 heterostructure. The as-prepared TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite possessed small particles, spherical-like shape, and anatase crystal form. Meanwhile, Rhodamine B and cefradine were chosen to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite. Results revealed that with the facile decoration of CQDs, the absorption of photocatalyst was extended into visible light region and photocatalytic activity was improved in comparison with pure TiO2. Furthermore, the mechanism for the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of the composites was discussed on the basis of the results. CQDs play an important role in the photocatalytic process, due to their superior ability to extend the visible absorption and produce more electrons and electron–hole pairs for the degradation of pollutants. In all, the paper offers further insights into the development of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite as photocatalyst for the degradation of antibiotics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2
CQDs/TiO2 composites
Photocatalytic degradation
Rhodamine B
Cefradine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs, TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 composite
The characterizations of CQDs, TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite
Photocatalytic degradation test on Rhodamine B
Condition optimization of photocatalysis for degrading cefradine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Calibration curve of RhB for uv-vis analysis, (b) cefradine for HPLC analysis.

SEM images of pure TiO2 (a) and CQDs/TiO2 composite (b); EDS analysis of the materials of (c) pure TiO2and (d) CQDs/TiO2 composite; (e) TEM analysis of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite(some CQDs were pointed out by the red circles); (f)TEM analysis of individual CQDs.
(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite;

RhB concentration changes(a) and cefradine concentration changes (b), with irradiation time over TiO2 without UV light and no catalyst, TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 composite prepared by different amount of CQDs under visible light irradiation (λ=365nm), respectively; (c) Plots of ln(C0/C) versus t of CQDs/TiO2 composite prepared by 20mg CQDs.

(a) Effect of initial concentration of cefradine on the photocatalytic activities of CQDs/TiO2 composite (inset: plots of degradation rates versus concentration of cefradine); (b) effect of amount of CQDs/TiO2 composite on its photocatalytic activities (inset: plots of degradation rates versus concentration of CQDs/TiO2 composite; (c) effect of pH of reaction solution on the photocatalytic activities of CQDs/TiO2 composite (inset: plots of degradation rates versus pH); (d) the changes of pH value before and after reaction during the reaction solution with different initial pH value; (e) effect of foreign inorganic ions on the photocatalytic activities of CQDs/TiO2 composite under the visible irradiation, respectively;

(a) The proposed mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of cefradine upon CQDs/TiO2 composite under UV–vis light irradiation; the effects of methanol and hydrogen peroxide on the degradation of cefradine; (c) the effect of different concentration of hydrogen peroxide on the photocatalytic process.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanocomposite for photo-degradation of Rhodamine B and cefradine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pure TiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-doped TiO2 nanocomposite (CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite) were prepared by a sol-gel approach for photocatalytic removal of Rhodamine B and cefradine. Analyses by Transmission electronmicroscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS), UV–visible spectroscopy and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) confirmed the successful formation of CQDs/TiO2 heterostructure. The as-prepared TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite possessed small particles, spherical-like shape, and anatase crystal form. Meanwhile, Rhodamine B and cefradine were chosen to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite. Results revealed that with the facile decoration of CQDs, the absorption of photocatalyst was extended into visible light region and photocatalytic activity was improved in comparison with pure TiO2. Furthermore, the mechanism for the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of the composites was discussed on the basis of the results. CQDs play an important role in the photocatalytic process, due to their superior ability to extend the visible absorption and produce more electrons and electron–hole pairs for the degradation of pollutants. In all, the paper offers further insights into the development of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite as photocatalyst for the degradation of antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most promising photocatalysts for large scale production, TiO2 has been widely studied during the decades on account of their low cost, favorable chemical stability and low toxicity [1]. The key step of photocatalytic process is to generate highly active photo-exited e−/h+ pairs and increase the utilization of photogenerated charge carriers for photocatalytic reaction. However, the band gap of TiO2 (anatase: 3.2eV; rutile: 3.0eV) irradiated only by 3–5% UV light of solar spectrum has limited its widespread application [2]. Various attempts have been made to modify TiO2 nanoparticles to enhance its photocatalytic activity by coupling with other materials, such as semiconductor materials, metal ions, dyes and nonmetal elements. These materials can form the energies of the intermediate states in the band gap [3,4]. N doped TiO2 was firstly reported to improve photocatalysis of TiO2 in the visible light range, and then follow by C, S, Fe and F elements [5–7]. Yet, the combination of charge carriers generated by TiO2 doped heteroatoms irradiated will be accelerated inside TiO2, due to the increase of massive binding sites introduced by heteroatoms and defects.

Carbon nanomaterials have been demonstrated as efficient means to enhance the photocatalytic reactions on account of their superior conductivity, capacitance,absorption of visible light and chemical stability. For instance, carbon-based TiO2 was prepared to degrade the cytotoxin released from cyanobacteria, such as microcystin-l, cylindrospermopsin, geosmin, and 2-methylisoborneol [8–10]. However, carbon nanotubes and graphene are easy to aggregate and inconvenient to react with charge carriers of TiO2, which will limit their photocatalysis application [11–13]. To avoid the aggregation, multi-walled carbon nanotubes coated with TiO2 were prepared by a sol-gel method and loaded on heat-pretreated silica gel for photocatalytic removal of NO. The result revealed that optimal denitration efficiency of MWCNTs/TiO2 was 46% [14]. Recently, Dionysiou group have developed ceramic membranes coated with partially reduced graphene oxide/TiO2 composite (GOT) to remove the organic dyes [15]. Results demonstrated that the photocatalytic membrane reactor has higher pollutant removal efficiency and lower energy consumption, comparing with standard nanofiltration. The team also utilized the TiO2-RGO composites for the disinfection of water under real sun conditions, which have achieved good results [16]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a kind of novel and environmental carbon nanomaterial, have been drawn much attention due to their multiple synthesis methods, favorable biocompatibility and unique photo-induced electron transfer [17]. Among the various applications of CQDs, some researchers have focused on the photocatalysis of CQDs. For instance, Au/CQDs composite were used as a photocatalyst for selective oxidation of cyclohexane in the presence of H2O2 under visible light [18]. Result showed that the conversion efficiency was 63.8% and selectivity was 99.9% for the oxidation of cyclohexane to cyclohexane. Similarly, CQDs can realize the transformation from benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde with high selectivity (100%) and conversion (92%) under NIR light irradiation [19]. As for the photocatalytic degradation of dye, CQDs doped with ZnO and HA respectively to degrade the methylene blue (MB) under visible-light irradiation [20,21]. Both of them revealed that the degradation efficiency of MB by composites reached the maximum value of 80%–90%, which enhanced by about 3 times with respect to that of single nanoparticles. Remarkably, CQDs have been embedded in various microstructures of TiO2, and then used to enhance the photocatalysis of degrading azo-dye, methylene blue and catalyzing hydrogen evolution in the previous studies [22–24]. Tian and his colleges reported good performance of CQDs/hydrogenated TiO2 in the degradation of methylene orange in aqueous solution upon UV, visible, and NIR light irradiation [22]. Moreover, CQDs/TiO2 composites also can be utilized for H2 evolution under UV–Vis light irradiation, which showed photocatalytic H2 evolution rate was 4 times higher than that of pure P25 [25]. These literatures proposed that CQDs with NIR up-conversion activity might serve as an intermedium to generate strongly oxidative holes and reductive electrons, which is the key factor in enhancing the photocatalytic activity [26].

In recent years, organic dyes and antibiotics which are important pollutants released from human activities, have been widely concerned by people. These environmental pollutions are persistent and bioaccumulative, and then will bring great damage to ecological environment if their concentrations greatly exceed the level of environment self-purification. So much effort has been made on the removal of organic dyes and antibiotics from water [27,28]. As for the degradation of antibiotics, pure TiO2 and metallic nanoparticles supported on TiO2 have been used to degrade antibiotic in water, showing auspicious results [29,30]. Giraldo-Aguirre and his colleagues reported the complete degradation of oxacillin after 120min of UV light (150W) irradiation using TiO2 nanoparticles [31]. Similarly, a previous report described the complete photodegradation of chloramphenicol after 18min of UV light irradiation using silver-doped TiO2 nanoparticles [32]. Chung et al. reported the complete degradation and mineralization of ciprofloxacin within 180min of UV-C light irradiation using TiO2 modified with mono-(Au, Ag and Cu) and bi-metallic (Au–Ag and Au–Cu) nanoparticles [33]. Though studies related to photocatalytic degradation on antibiotics using pure TiO2 and TiO2 doped metallic nanoparticles are cited in the literature, the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics using CQDs/TiO2 composite has been rarely reported [29,34].

Herein, we demonstrated a convenient sol-gel method to synthesize TiO2 doped CQDs heterostructure for enhancing the photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B and cefradine which is a semi-synthetic first-generation cephalosporin antibiotic used in human and veterinary medicine worldwide. Meanwhile, optimized parameters of photocatalytic degradation on cefradine and mechanism of photocatalytic degradation were also investigated. For the best of our knowledge, this is the first study using CQDs deposited on TiO2 for the removal of cefradine in water, which broadened the photocatalytic application of CQDs/TiO2 composite.

Hydrogen peroxide (30%, Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., AR) and acetic acid (Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., AR ) used to synthesize the CQDs were purchased from Nanjing chemical reagent corporation. Tetrabutyl titanate (98%, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., AR) and nitric acid (65–68%, Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., AR) were used to synthesize TiO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composite materials. Methanol (HPLC) and acetonitrile (HPLC) were purchased from CINC High Purity Solvents (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. The other reagents including Rhodamin B (RhB), cefradine and dehydrated alcohol were analytical grade reagents and used as received. High-purity water was used in the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs, TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared by chemical oxidation treatment of starch according to our previous report. Briefly, carbonized starch as carbon source was dissolved in the distilled water. Then the solution was refluxed with acetic acid and hydrogen peroxide based on certain scale (1:1:2) for 2h at 120°C [35]. Finally, the CQDs were obtained after the purification by dialysis. To synthesize CQDs/TiO2 composite, 56mL ethanol was added slowly into 14mL CQDs aqueous solution (dissolved 10mg, 20mg, 40mg CQDs powder respectively) under stirring [36]. Then the solution was adjusted to pH=2.5 with HNO3 and put into a round-bottomed flask (250mL), follow by water bath heating at 70°C with the mechanical stirring. 8.5mL tetrabutyl titanate was injected into the reaction solution and heated for 24h. After that, the crude products were washed with absolute ethanol and distilled water for several times and then dried in an oven at 80°C for 8h. The synthetic process of TiO2 was the same as that of CQDs/TiO2 except that CQDs were not used.

Transmission electronmicroscopy (TEM) was carried out using JEM-2100 operated at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of catalysts were collected on a ARL X'TRA X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation in the 2θ range of 15–85°C. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to characterize the morphologies and size of the synthesized TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite. The chemical composition was investigated via energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). UV–vis spectroscopy was carried out with a Shimadzu UV-2100 UV/vis spectrometer. HPLC (BACKMAN Spectra 100) was used to measure the concentration of cefradine. The detailed chromatographic conditions were as follow. The sample was eluted by the buffer solution of acetonitrile and acetic acid (12:88) with constant liquid flow rates (1.0mL/min) to go through a chromatographic column (Baseline C18 (150×4.6nm, 5μm). XPA photocatalytic reactor (Xujiang Machine Factory, Nanjing, China) was used for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB and cefradine [37].

The photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/TiO2 composite materials was investigated by the photodegradation of Rhodamin B (RhB) and cefradine under the irradiation of an 8W UV lamp(λ=365nm). For degradation of RhB, 1mg/mL solution of CQDs/TiO2 composite synthesized by various amounts of CQDs(10mg, 20mg, 40mg) were added in 200mL RhB (5mg/L) solution respectively. Then, the suspensions were treated by ultrasound for 10min to ensure the photocatalyst was fully contacted with RhB. After that, the solution was exposed to UV-irradiation under stirring (400rpm) at 20°C for 120min. During the measurement process, a certain amount of the solution was sampled at certain time intervals and centrifuged (10,000rpm, 3min) to remove the photocatalyst particles. The supernatant was analyzed to measure the concentration of RhB by using a Shimadzu UV-2100 UV/vis spectrometer (peak center: 554nm). The calibration curve presents a linear relation within the range of 0.1–10mg/L and relationship coefficient was 0.9998 (Fig. 1(a)). Eventually, the conversion was defined as follows:
conversion
%
=
C 0
− C
C 0
× 100
where C0 is the initial concentration of RhB and Cr is the concentration of reactant, respectively.

For degradation of cefradine, the photocatalytic process was the same as that of RhB. Briefly, 1mg/mL solution of CQDs/TiO2 composite were added in 200mL cefradine (10mg/L) solution. Then the solution is loaded in the XPA photocatalytic reactor, under the irradiation of an 8W UV lamp (λ=365nm) for 60min. The concentration of cefradine was determined by HPLC at the wavelength of 254nm using the calibration curve determined in advance (Fig. 1(b)). External standard method was used for the quantitative analysis of cefradine. The calibration curve presents a linear relation within the range of 0.1–10mg/L and relationship coefficient was 0.9998(Fig. 1 (a)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterizations of CQDs, TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and elemental composition of the CQDs, TiO2 as well as CQDs/TiO2 microspheres were observed by SEM, TEM and EDS analysis (Fig. 2 ). The SEM images (Fig. 2(a–b)) revealed that both TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite were in spherical-like shape, well crystal structure, but with obvious aggregation. As shown in Fig. 2(c–d),The EDS spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 composite (Fig. 2(d)) shows three peaks which were identified as carbon (14.42%), oxygen (45.97%) and titanium (39.62%), while the EDS spectrum of TiO2 only shows two peaks of titanium (51.99%)and oxygen(48.01%), indicating that CQDs successfully was doped into TiO2. Furthermore, TEM analysis was carried out to identify the presence of carbon dots in the TiO2 (Fig. 2e–f). As showed in the Fig. 2f, the individual CQDs were spherical-like shape particles with the diameter of 3–8nm, while Fig. 2e showed that CQDs can be observed in the surface of TiO2, indicating that the CQDs had been embedded into the TiO2 to generate the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite.

In order to reveal the functions of CQDs, TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 composite, their UV–vis absorption spectra were studied in detail. As shown in Fig. 3(a), pure CQDs and TiO2, respectively. The strong absorptions of CQDs, TiO2 were showed in the UV region (214nm), UV–vis region (314nm), UV–vis region (304nm), especially. As for the UV–vis spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 composite, the shape of peak was similar to that of TiO2, while the absorption intensity in the range of 300–800nm was much stronger than that of TiO2 [38]. The result indicated that CQDs may contribute to improve the optical activity of TiO2, which may result in more electron–hole pairs. The XRD patterns of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite are displayed in Fig. 3, which have obvious characteristic diffraction peaks and is in good agreement with the standard data for anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21-1272) by the exposure of 25.28°, 37.80°, 48.05°, 53.89°and 55.06° peaks, corresponding to the reflections from (101), (004), (200), (205) and (211) crystal planes, respectively. It revealed that CQDs not only can improve the absorption intensity of TiO2, but maintain the good crystalline character and phase purity of the resultant products, which are probably attributed to the poor crystallinity and low content of CQDs. The result was in conformity with the relative references. In brief, the results of UV–vis spectrum and XRD showed that the stacking structure of Ti and O in the complex belongs to TiO2 anatase, and the integrity crystal structure of CQDs/TiO2 composite [24,39].
(b) XRD patterns of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation test on Rhodamine B
ce:section.ce:para: Rhodamine B is an organic dye that has been widely employed in biochemical analysis, paper industry and cosmetic. CQDs/TiO2 composite were used as the catalyst for the degradation of RhB. 1mg/mL solution of CQDs/TiO2 composite synthesized by various amounts of CQDs(10mg, 20mg, 40mg) were added in 200mL RhB (5mg/L) solution respectively. Then the photocatalytic activities of CQDs/TiO2 composite were investigated. During the process, a certain amount of the solution was sampled at certain time intervals to measure the concentration of RhB, and Fig. 4(a) shows the photodegradation efficiency of RhB exposed to CQDs/TiO2 composite. The result reveals that the adsorption rate of TiO2 with RhB was 15.07% in the dark and the degradation rate of RhB under the UV light irradiation, while photodegradation efficiency of RhB exposed to CQDs/TiO2 composite had been increased to 74.08%, indicating that as-prepared TiO2 showed significant catalytic effect on the degradation of RhB. Remarkably, the degradation rates of RhB by CQDs/TiO2 composite prepared with different amount of the added CQDs (10mg, 20mg, 40mg) were 84.74%,91.28%,86.92%, respectively. The result highlighted that the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composite was significantly higher than that of TiO2, which was in conformity with the relative reference. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 composite prepared by 20mg CQDs was the highest during the degradation of RhB.

The photocatalytic activities of CQDs/TiO2 composite synthesized by various amounts of CQDs (10mg, 20mg, and 40mg) to the 200mL cefradine (10mg/L) were investigated respectively. A certain amount of the solution was sampled at several points during the photocatalysis. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the degradation rate of cefradine under the UV light or reacted with CQDs/TiO2 composite in the dark were very low. Similar to the photocatalytic degradation test on RhB, the result also shows that photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composite was higher than that of TiO2, indicating that the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/TiO2 composite toward cefradine might be consistent with toward RhB. Notably, CQDs/TiO2 composite synthesized by 20mg CQDs had the strongest photocatalytic activity, thus 20mg CQDs/TiO2 composite were selected as the optimum photocatalyst.

Kinetics of photocatalytic degradation of cefradine by CQDs/TiO2 composite was investigated using Langmuir-Hinshelwood dynamic model (Fig. 4(b)) [40].
(1)
dc / dt = kKc /
1 + Kc
where c is the concentration at any time; k and K are the limiting rate constants of reaction at maximum coverage under the given experimental conditions and equilibrium constant.

When concentration of substrate was much less than 1, the Eq. (1) can be simplified to pseudo-first-order kinetic equation.
(2)
− ln
C 0 / C
= kt
where C0 and C are the equilibrium concentration of adsorption and the concentration of RhB at their radiation time, t, respectively, and k is the apparent rate constant.
The result showed that the photocatalytic degradation of cefradine by CQDs/TiO2 composite was in accordance with pseudo first-order kinetics, and the degradation rate constant was 0.02585min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization of photocatalysis for degrading cefradine
ce:section.ce:para: In order to improve the degradation efficiency of cefradine by CQDs/TiO2 composite, the conditions of photocatalytic degradation such as the initial concentration of cefradine, amount of catalyst, and pH value of reaction liquid were optimized(Fig. 5 (a–c)). Besides, the interference of foreign inorganic ions was also investigated, as shown in the Fig. 5(d). The degradation efficiency of initial concentration of cefradine ranging from 5.0 to 50.0mg/L by CQDs/TiO2 composite (1g/L) was studied (Fig. 5(a)). The degradation rate constants decreased with the initial concentration of cefradine increased. It was speculated that the cefradine absorbed on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite increased with the increase of concentration. However, excessive cefradine may hamper the composite material to receive UV light to activate the photocatalytic processes. Fig. 5(b) shows the effect of different concentration of CQDs/TiO2 composite on the photocatalytic activity. The concentration of cefradine was 20.0mg/L. The degradation rate constants were positively corresponded with the concentration of photocatalyst in the scope of 0–2.0g/L, and processed to decline with the highest efficiency at proper concentration (2.0g/L). It may result from increases in turbidity of reaction solution with increased concentration of photocatalyst, and therefore the degradation efficiency decreased with the decline of UV transmittance.

Fig. 5(c) shows the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of cefradine (20.0mg/L) by with CQDs/TiO2 composite (1g/L) various pH values at 2.85, 4.56, 7.55, 9.46, and 11.37. The lone pair electrons of S and N atoms contained in cefradine can adsorb protons easily to make cefradine appear alkaline. Thus, the existential state of cefradine changed with the initial pH of reaction solution, which resulted in different degradation degree. It was observed that the degradation degree achieved the maximum at pH value 2.85 during the acid range, while reached its maximum at pH value 9.46 when pH value is higher than pH7. The degradation of cefradine in the acid range may attribute to the instability of cefradine in acidic condition, so that it can accelerate the photodegradation. Additionally, Fig. 5(d) shows that the initial pH of reaction liquid was 7.55 (the original pH value of the solution without adjustment), it had no obvious decrease during the photodegradation, while the initial pH was 9.46, the value had decreased significantly. It might be attributable to OH– absorbed onto CQDs/TiO2 composite, because of the composite with acid activity. The result not only correspond to the Fig. 5(c), but previous literature showed that the ∙OH radicals can be generated by OH−, giving rise to a higher photocatalytic performance [41].

The effects of foreign inorganic ions on the photocatalytic degradation of cefradine were also investigated (Fig. 5(e)). The NH4 + and NO3 − were selected on account of N atom contained in cefradine. According to previous study, NH4 + can react with h+ and consume the h+ generated in the photo-catalysis process, which result in the downtrend of degradation [42].

However, the result shown in Fig. 5(e) illustrated that both of NH4+ and NO3− had almost no effect on the photocatalysis, which may respectively due to the OH– on the surface of photocatalyst can hinder the attachment of NH4 +, and NO3 − of high oxidation state was difficult to be oxidized which resulted in the depletion of e− and h+.

The photocatalytic mechanism of the as-prepared CQDs/TiO2 composite for the degradation of cefradine was further explored. According to the previous literature, photocatalysis can be excited effectively as long as the wavelength is below 480nm, thus ultraviolet light (365nm) was selected as light sources [43]. Based on the experimental result in this system and literature references, the photocatalytic mechanism was proposed (Fig. 6 ) [44]. For the CQDs/TiO2 system (Fig. 6(a)), the electrons can be excited from TiO2 when it was irradiated by ultraviolet light. Then, the electron could transfer to CQDs and result in efficient separation of electron–hole pairs (h+). The photogenerated electron and hole pairs then react with the adsorbed OH– to produce active OH radicals (OH) which was a strong oxidizing agent to decompose cefradine [45,46]. This explained why CQDs decorated into the TiO2 spheres can enhance the photocatalytic activity.

In order to prove the participation of OH in photocatalysis, 1.5mol/L methanol was added to reaction liquid because methanol can react with OH radicals. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the addition of methanol can slow down the degradation, which fully proved the appearance of (OH) during the photocatalysis and explained the phenomena that the degradation of cefradine can be enhanced in the weak basic condition[47]. On the other hand, the electron transferred to the surface of composite could also react with O2 dissolved in solution to generate hydroxyl (OH) and possible others(O2 - or HOO), thus led to degrade the pollutant effectively [48,49]. Hydrogen peroxide (electron acceptor) was added to the reaction solution to verify above corollary (as show in Fig. 6(c)) [50]. The degradation was strengthened gradually with the increase of hydrogen peroxide in effective concentration range (1–5mol/L), due to hydrogen peroxide of strong oxidizing property to trap electrons.
H2O2 +e− → OH+OH–.
OH+H2O2 →H2O+ HO2
OH+ HO2 →H2O+O2

The consumption of e- during the photocatalytic process can contribute to generate OH and therefore degradation was strengthened. But when the concentration increased to a certain value, the efficiency will decrease when the concentration of the H2O2 increased, which was in agreement with previous report [51]. This was due to excessive H2O2 adsorbed on the surface of photocatalyst may inhibit the aggregation of cefradine on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 composite [37]. It was demonstrated that excessive H2O2 could scavenge the OH through the above equation, resulting in the decrease of photocatalytic degradation [50–52]. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composite was higher than pure TiO2 may attribute to more electrons and electron–hole pairs can be produced from excited composite when it was irradiated by ultraviolet light, which was consistent with Fig. 3(a).

In summary, CQDs synthesized by chemical oxidation were facilely reacted with tetrabutyl titanate via a simple sol-gel method to form a CQDs/TiO2 heterostructure, which possess higher photocatalytic activity under UV-light irradiation compared to TiO2. The as-prepared CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 composite were characterized by ultraviolet-visible spectrum, TEM, SEM-EDS and XRD. The UV–vis absorption spectrum indicated that the absorption intensity of CQDs/TiO2 composite was much stronger than that of TiO2 on account of carbon quantum dots. The results of TEM, SEM-EDS and XRD showed that both of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite possessed small particles, spherical-like shape, and anatase crystal form. Then favorable photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composite for the degradation of RhB and cefradine were investigated. The result showed that CQDs play key roles in improving the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/TiO2 composite, and CQDs/TiO2 composite synthesized by 20mg CQDs has the highest photocatalytic activity to degrade of RhB and cefradine. Optimization experiments were also carried out to improve the photocatalysis of CQDs/TiO2 composite. Moreover, the possible mechanism was proposed that CQDs can help to improve the photogenerated electron–hole separation efficiency and absorb longer wavelength light for photocatalytic reaction. In all, the present work provides further insights into the development of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite as photocatalyst for the antibiotics degradation under UV-light irradiations for green chemical industry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by The National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (21507165), the fund of College Students Innovation Project for the R&D of Novel Drugs under Grant (No. J1030830) and the National College Students' Innovation Entrepreneurial Training Program (No. G15019).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of single-walled carbon nanotube/TiO2 hybrid atmospheric gas sensor operated at ambient temperature

Nanoscale
